cards.title: Vectorized Execution Explained
cards.teaser: DuckDB processes thousands of rows at once per operation — not one row at a time — and that single change explains most of its speed advantage.

runs.text: Traditional SQL engines use the "volcano model" (also called iterator model): each operator calls
on its child, which returns one row, which is passed up the pipeline. Elegant, but expensive — one function call per row, per operator, on millions of rows adds up to billions of function calls.
: each operator processes a batch of rows — by default 2,048 rows per vector — in a single call. One

items.text: values fits in L2 cache. Processing it entirely before moving on keeps the data hot.

Modern CPUs (x86 AVX2/AVX-512, ARM NEON) can perform arithmetic on 4–16 values simultaneously. Tight numeric loops over a contiguous array allow the compiler and CPU to auto-vectorize — one instruction, multiple data points.
Operator dispatch happens once per 2,048 rows, not once per row.
Null checks and type dispatches happen at the vector boundary, not inline per row.

runs.text: query, DuckDB issues roughly 4,883 vector calls. The volcano model issues 10,000,000 row-level calls. The arithmetic alone makes vectorized execution orders of magnitude cheaper for scan-heavy analytics.
The vector size of 2,048 rows is tunable via
, but the default is well-calibrated for modern L2 cache sizes. Changing it rarely improves real-world performance without profiling first.

Like the difference between processing a batch of images in one GPU kernel call versus submitting each image as a separate CPU task — the overhead of dispatch dominates when the unit of work is too small.

cards.title: Columnar Storage and Compression
cards.teaser: Storing data column-by-column lets DuckDB compress aggressively and skip entire column chunks without reading them — the layout is inseparable from the performance.

runs.text: Row-oriented storage (PostgreSQL, SQLite) writes each row contiguously:
, the engine reads every byte of every row to extract just the
column — skipping every other column entirely.
Two performance benefits fall directly from this layout:
Values in the same column are the same type and often correlated. A
repeated millions of times compresses to near nothing with dictionary encoding. A

column of floats near the same magnitude compresses well with frame-of-reference encoding. Row storage mixes types and disrupts these patterns — columnar storage enables compression rates of 5–20x on typical analytical data.

If a query references 3 out of 50 columns, DuckDB reads 3 column segments from disk. The other 47 are never accessed. On a 10GB table with 50 columns, a 3-column query might read 600MB instead of 10GB.
DuckDB uses several encoding schemes automatically:

items.text: Frame-of-reference for numeric columns with small ranges

runs.text: to Parquet and querying Parquet directly often produces better performance than equivalent CSV queries — the column layout and compression do the work before the query even runs.
Like a spreadsheet where you store each column in a separate array — computing a column sum becomes a tight loop over contiguous memory rather than a scattered read across a row-interleaved heap.

cards.title: Zone Maps and Predicate Pushdown
cards.teaser: DuckDB tracks min/max values per column chunk and uses them to skip entire blocks of data before evaluating any rows — a technique that makes WHERE clauses much cheaper.

runs.text: of 122,880 rows (the default). For each row group, it stores a
: the minimum and maximum value of that column chunk.
When a query has a filter predicate, DuckDB consults the zone maps before reading any data:
zone map for a row group shows
, the entire row group is skipped without reading a single row. On a sorted or partially sorted column, this eliminates the majority of I/O for range queries.
— filters are pushed down to the storage layer and evaluated as early as possible, before data is decoded and materialized.
How to maximize zone map effectiveness:

items.text: Sort your data on filter columns.
before writing. This concentrates relevant rows into fewer row groups.
column gets better zone map benefit than a
column containing date strings — string comparisons work but numeric comparisons are more precise.

runs.text: For large Parquet files you query repeatedly by date range, pre-sorting on the date column before writing can reduce query time by 80–90% on selective filters. Use
COPY (SELECT * FROM tbl ORDER BY event_date) TO 'sorted.parquet'
Like a book index — instead of reading every page to find mentions of a term, you consult a structure that tells you exactly which pages to skip.

cards.title: Parallel Query Execution
cards.teaser: DuckDB automatically splits scans, joins, and aggregations across all available CPU cores — parallelism is on by default and requires no query hints.

runs.text: DuckDB parallelizes query execution at the operator level across all available CPU cores. This happens automatically — no
hints, no partitioning declarations, no explicit configuration required for the default behavior.
How parallelism works per operator type:
The row group list is divided across threads. With 8 cores and 100 row groups, each thread scans ~12–13 row groups independently.
Each thread aggregates its partition of rows into a local hash table. Thread-local results are merged in a final reduction step. This avoids write contention on a shared hash table.

The build phase (loading the smaller table into a hash table) is parallelized. The probe phase (scanning the larger table and probing the hash table) is also parallelized across threads with a shared read-only hash table.
Parallel sort uses a split-then-merge strategy similar to external merge sort, with each thread sorting its partition independently before merging.

explanation.code:
-- Check current setting
SELECT current_setting('threads');

-- Set for the session
SET threads = 4;

-- Set at connection time (Python)
con = duckdb.connect()
con.execute("SET threads = 4")

runs.text: DuckDB defaults to the number of logical CPU cores. On a shared machine or container with constrained CPU quotas, set
On CI runners or containers with cgroup CPU limits, DuckDB may detect the physical core count of the host rather than the cgroup limit. Always set
explicitly in constrained environments to match your actual CPU budget.
Like map-reduce at the single-node level — each core runs a local map phase, then a shared reduce phase merges the partial results.

cards.title: The Query Optimizer
cards.teaser: DuckDB's optimizer uses cardinality statistics and a dynamic programming join order search — it makes better join ordering decisions than most heuristic-based systems.

runs.text: DuckDB's query optimizer operates in several stages before execution:

items.text: Predicate pushdown — moves filters as close to the scan as possible.
Projection pushdown — eliminates unused columns early.
Common subexpression elimination — avoids recomputing identical expressions.
Filter and join reordering — uses selectivity estimates to order filters cheaply-first.

runs.text: for queries with up to a configurable number of tables (default 10). For queries with more tables it falls back to a greedy algorithm. The DP optimizer considers all possible join orders and selects the one with the lowest estimated cost — significantly better than left-deep heuristics used by many simpler systems.
The optimizer uses column statistics (min, max, distinct count, null fraction) gathered during writes and
to estimate cardinalities. More accurate statistics → better join order decisions.

DuckDB can switch between hash join and nested loop join mid-query based on observed row counts. If a join's build side turns out to be larger than estimated, the optimizer adapts rather than committing to a bad plan upfront.

explanation.code:
ANALYZE;           -- update statistics for all tables
ANALYZE my_table;  -- update for one table

runs.text: On tables that receive large batch inserts, run
before the next complex query. Stale statistics are the most common cause of unexpectedly slow queries in DuckDB.
Like a chess engine evaluating positions several moves ahead — the DP optimizer exhaustively searches the space of join orderings rather than committing to the first reasonable-looking plan.

cards.title: Reading EXPLAIN Output
cards.teaser: EXPLAIN shows the physical query plan DuckDB will execute — reading it takes five minutes to learn and saves hours of guessing why a query is slow.

runs.text: DuckDB supports two forms of query plan inspection:
Key operators to recognize in EXPLAIN output:

items.text: — sequential column scan. Expected for analytical queries. Shows which columns were read and any filters pushed to the scan.
— a filter not pushed to the scan (e.g., applied after a join). If you see FILTER above a SEQ_SCAN on a selective predicate, the optimizer may be missing statistics.
— standard hash join. Shows build side (smaller table) and probe side. Check estimated vs actual row counts.
— parallel aggregation via hash table. Expected for GROUP BY.
— sort operation. Expensive on large tables — confirm it is necessary.
Large estimated vs actual row count mismatch
FILTER above SEQ_SCAN on a selective column
— predicate pushdown is not happening; check if the filter uses a function on the column.
— may indicate the optimizer chose a suboptimal plan due to bad statistics.
— sorting is often the bottleneck for queries that do not need full ordering; consider

runs.text: actually runs the query. Use it on queries you are already running — do not run it on long queries just to inspect the plan, as you will pay the full execution cost.
Like reading a compiler's assembly output — most of the time you do not need it, but when a hot path is unexpectedly slow, it is the fastest way to understand what the machine is actually doing.

cards.title: Memory Management and the Buffer Pool
cards.teaser: DuckDB manages its own memory pool with a configurable limit — understanding how it uses memory prevents OOM surprises on shared machines.

runs.text: to manage memory for query execution. All intermediate results, hash tables, sort buffers, and column data in flight pass through this pool.
. On a laptop with 16GB RAM that means DuckDB will use up to ~12.8GB before taking action. On a shared CI runner with 4GB of RAM shared across processes, this default is almost always wrong.
Or at connection time in Python:
What DuckDB tracks toward the memory limit:

items.text: Column data loaded into the buffer pool
Hash tables for joins and aggregations
Python objects that reference DuckDB results (after
, the data is in Python's heap, not DuckDB's)
Arrow zero-copy results (memory is shared, counted once)

runs.text: This returns a breakdown of memory used by category — useful when debugging unexpectedly high consumption.
lower than the default is safe and does not degrade correctness — DuckDB will spill to disk when the limit is reached. Set it to leave headroom for other processes, especially in containers.
Like setting a JVM heap size with
— the runtime needs an explicit ceiling or it will consume as much as the OS allows, which is often more than a shared environment can afford.

cards.title: Spilling to Disk When Memory Is Exhausted
cards.teaser: When a query exceeds the memory limit, DuckDB spills intermediate results to temporary files on disk — the query still completes, but slower.

runs.text: DuckDB does not crash or error when a query exceeds
intermediate data (hash tables, sort buffers) to a temporary directory on disk, processes from disk, and then cleans up afterward. This is called

items.text: — the build-side hash table is partitioned and the overflow written to temp files.
— partial aggregation hash tables spill when full.
— external merge sort writes sorted runs to disk and merges them.

runs.text: Operations that do not spill (and will error if memory is insufficient):

items.text: Window functions with very large frames

runs.text: Default is the same directory as the database file (or the OS temp directory for in-memory databases). If spilling is expected, point this at a fast local NVMe drive rather than a network-mounted volume.
annotations on hash join and hash group-by operators. If you see spilling on a query that runs frequently:

items.text: if the machine has spare RAM.
Reduce the query's intermediate data (push filters earlier, reduce join fanout).

runs.text: Spilling to a network filesystem (NFS, EFS, SMB) can make a query 100x slower than the same query on a local disk. Always configure
to a local path if you expect memory-intensive queries.
Like a database sort that pages through a B-tree when it cannot fit the sort key in RAM — it still produces correct results, just with disk I/O replacing in-memory operations.

cards.title: The .duckdb File Format
cards.teaser: DuckDB's file format stores data in a columnar block structure with a catalog, row groups, and a checkpoint mechanism — knowing the layout explains WAL behavior and file sizes.

runs.text: file is a self-contained binary file with several internal layers:

items.text: A file header with a magic number (
segment storing table definitions, indexes, and schema metadata.
— the actual columnar data, organized into row groups of 122,880 rows each.
Each column segment stores compressed column chunks with accompanying zone maps (min/max metadata).

runs.text: DuckDB writes changes in memory first, then periodically
— serializing the in-memory state to the

items.text: After a configurable number of WAL writes (

runs.text: Before data is checkpointed to the main file, it is appended to a
file. This ensures crash recovery — on next open, DuckDB replays the WAL to recover any un-checkpointed writes.
files do not automatically shrink after deletes. Run
to reclaim space from deleted rows. Run
to rewrite and compact the file (takes a write lock for the duration).
files must always travel together. Copying the
file (if one exists) may produce a database that is missing recent writes. Copy both, or use
Like Git's object store and ref log — the main file is the stable committed state, the WAL is the in-flight transaction log, and a checkpoint is the equivalent of a garbage-collected pack operation.

cards.title: Benchmarking DuckDB Queries
cards.teaser: EXPLAIN ANALYZE gives per-operator timing and row counts — but accurate benchmarking requires warm-up runs, realistic data sizes, and measuring the right thing.

explanation.code:
EXPLAIN ANALYZE
SELECT region, SUM(revenue), COUNT(*)
FROM orders
WHERE order_date >= '2025-01-01'
GROUP BY region
ORDER BY SUM(revenue) DESC;

items.text: Estimated vs actual row counts per operator
Whether any operator spilled to disk

runs.text: DuckDB tracks query timing automatically. After any query,
returns the most recent profiling data:

items.text: Run the query once before timing — the first run loads column chunks into the buffer pool. Subsequent runs are faster. If benchmarking cold-start I/O performance, clear the OS page cache between runs.
DuckDB's vectorized execution has overhead that only pays off at scale. A 10,000-row table benchmarks worse relative to SQLite than a 10,000,000-row table.
overhead. Time the SQL execution separately:
to isolate query time from result materialization time.
to force evaluation without materializing the full result set.
DuckDB caches parsed queries and compiled pipelines. The second run of an identical query is sometimes faster than the first by 5–15% due to pipeline caching.

runs.text: For macro-benchmarks comparing DuckDB configurations (different thread counts, memory limits, file formats), use the TPC-H or TPC-DS benchmark datasets — they are available as DuckDB shell scripts via the
Like profiling a web server with
— a single-run timing number is almost meaningless; percentiles across warm, repeated runs with realistic load reveal the actual behavior.

cards.title: Pipelines and the Push-Based Execution Model
cards.teaser: DuckDB uses a push-based pipeline execution model internally — understanding it explains why some query shapes are faster and how parallel execution is structured.

runs.text: (also called "morsel-driven") model rather than the pull-based volcano iterator model.
In the pull model: the top operator pulls rows from its children — control flows top-down, data flows bottom-up via function return values.
In the push model: operators are compiled into
— chains of operators that can be executed with a single pass over the data. The source (a scan) pushes vectors through the pipeline until it hits a
— an operation that must see all input before producing output.

items.text: Hash joins (must build the full hash table before probing)
A pipeline with no breakers can be parallelized trivially — split the scan across threads, each thread runs the full pipeline on its partition.
Pipeline-parallel execution avoids thread synchronization until a breaker is reached.
DuckDB compiles each pipeline into a tight execution loop. Column vectors flow through filter, project, and expression operators without intermediate materialization.

explanation.code:
SELECT region, SUM(price * quantity)
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'shipped'
GROUP BY region

items.text: Scan → Filter → Project → Build hash aggregation table (breaker)
Finalize and return hash aggregation results

runs.text: Each pipeline runs parallel across all threads, synchronizing only at the breaker boundary.

Like an assembly line where each station passes the part to the next without stopping — the pipeline flows continuously until it hits an operation (like quality inspection) that requires accumulating the full batch.

cards.title: Adaptive Radix Tree Indexes
cards.teaser: DuckDB supports ART (Adaptive Radix Tree) indexes for point lookups — they help specific query patterns but are rarely needed for analytical workloads.

runs.text: indexes — a trie-based data structure that provides O(k) lookup time (where k is key length) and compact memory usage compared to B-trees.

explanation.code:
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_id ON orders(id);
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx_users_email ON users(email);

items.text: Range scans on highly selective predicates (returning < 0.1% of rows)

runs.text: When ART indexes do not help (and add overhead):

items.text: Full table scans — DuckDB always does a full scan regardless of index presence for non-selective predicates
Aggregations over large fractions of a table
Analytical queries that read most of the column data anyway

runs.text: The overhead of maintaining an index:

items.text: Index entries are updated on every INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE — write-heavy tables with indexes are slower to load
Indexes consume memory proportional to the number of indexed rows

runs.text: zone maps (automatic) and column pruning are more impactful than explicit indexes
. Add an index only after confirming via
that a non-selective point lookup is the actual bottleneck.

DuckDB does not require indexes for aggregation performance — zone maps and columnar layout do that work. Treat ART indexes as a targeted tool for point lookup patterns, not a general performance default.
Like adding an index to a database table in PostgreSQL — it speeds up lookups at the cost of write overhead, but for full-table analytical scans it provides no benefit and just adds maintenance cost.

cards.title: Profiling with duckdb_profiling_output
cards.teaser: DuckDB's built-in profiling system captures per-operator timing and cardinality data without external tools — enabling systematic performance diagnosis inside a single SQL session.

runs.text: DuckDB has a built-in profiling mode that captures detailed execution statistics for the most recently run query.
The JSON output includes a tree of operators with the following per-node fields:

items.text: — rows produced by this operator (after filtering)
— wall-clock time spent in this operator
— operator-specific details (e.g., columns scanned, join keys, filter expressions)
on a SEQ_SCAN indicates a selective filter that is not using zone maps. Check if the filter column has sorted data or if it could benefit from an index.
relative to probe side indicates the build table is larger than expected — check join direction.
on hash operators means memory limit was hit — increase limit or tune the query.

runs.text: Profiling adds ~2–5% overhead per query. Leave it disabled in production code paths and enable it only during active investigation.
profiling format is machine-readable and can be parsed with
directly in DuckDB for automated performance monitoring pipelines.
— the engine captures detailed timing data internally during execution, and you read it as structured output rather than instrumenting your code externally.
